next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

The lights failed hard at 21:17.

Not the soft brown shiver the corridor had been living with all month, not the blink that took one row of carriage lamps and gave them back with a complaint from the ballast. This cut took the fans, the badge readers, the bilingual courtesy in the announcement loop, and for one long second the train seemed to forget what shape it was supposed to hold. The floor vibration dropped out under Marisol's soles. Somewhere forward a child started crying before the emergency strips came up red and mean along the baseboards.

She was in the sanitation corridor between Car 7 and the trauma module with both hands inside an access box, feeling for the manual pump switch that never sat where the diagram said it sat. When the power died, the blackwater line gave a thick backward gulp. She heard it through the pipe before she smelled it. A second later the smell hit: hot sewage, enzyme foam, old metal cooked all day and suddenly opened.

The radio on her shoulder cracked with three voices at once. Manual count. Stay where you are. All movement suspended pending tier verification. Custody wanted every car sealed. Dispatch wanted a systems assessment. Somebody in Spanish was swearing about the south lavs backing up. The synthetic announcement tried to come back and managed only Please rem before cutting into static.

Marisol got the pump lever down, felt the line catch halfway, and understood two things together. First: the outage was regional, bigger than one train, because the signal relay should have jumped to reserve by now and had not. Sec...
open_hooks_to_carry: Stage the pathogen gambit through procedure and timing, not melodramatic villainy.
Make the death physically and operationally legible.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d the service corridor, the train had shifted its weight on the track. It was not moving yet. It was only preparing. The floor vibration changed pitch under her soles, a subtle gathering. She stood for one moment beside the waste drum rack and let the metal talk through her legs.

She ran through it again: shared track, outside dispatch, public contamination, wrong key count, names paired to objects, water for five-A, pencils, recorder batteries, Paloma's cooler, Ivo's bad numbers, Naila's wrapper knots, Gabriel's voice, dry as paper until it wasn't.

Faith was too polished a word for the room. The room held a medic whose housing sat inside her contract, a mechanic counting overtime against child-support arrears, a detainee who would not take private mercy, a girl with missing fingernails tying witness into plastic loops, and Marisol herself, who knew better than any of them how easily the institution swallowed mess when the mess stayed private. The plan only existed if each of them did the part that could ruin them. Preparation and betrayal now lay close enough together to cross in a single bad minute.

She put her hand over the downgraded passcard, feeling the plastic heat against her sternum, and started walking toward the sanitation bay. There was still time before dawn, before quarterly reconciliation closed the files into harder lies, but not much. Enough time to steal water and pencils, enough time to separate fresh batteries for the recorder, enough time to meet Paloma in the trauma room before the yard fully woke, and enough time for nerve if nobody spent it early.
state_deltas_required: Internal surveillance, rushed transfer orders, and a sabotage attempt that depends on perfect timing.
The quarantine plan backfires, an elderly detainee dies, Paloma is exposed, and Marisol's sabotage gains an irreversible human cost.
